w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: 4.7 APPLIANCE DETAILS (ADJUST AS APPLICABLE FOR APPLIANCES)

Disclaimer: Systems or products mentioned in this document may not bear the trademarks of their Vendors or Owners. Herein, it is acknowledged that all the trademarks are the property of their respective owners. It is also acknowledged that there is no intention of breaching anybody's copyrights, if there are extractions from any vendors' documents.
JavaScript UI library for building MFE portals
JavaScript module bundler with Module Federation
Library for reading/writing Excel XLSX files

The objective of this document is to provide low-level technical details of the solution for Cognizant / Unified Rewards Platform (URP). This Low Level Design (LLD) document, as well as all deliverables produced as part of this project, are considered a living document. Revisions are expected and shall be made during the review process with Cognizant stakeholders, as well as in subsequent phases if it is discovered that a process or design can be enhanced to benefit the overall outcome of the project.

This document provides granular technical specifications for: component configurations, service-level design decisions, database schemas, port matrices, service accounts, monitoring thresholds, HA/DR strategies, and capacity planning for the Unified Rewards Platform deployed on Microsoft Azure.
The following technical solutions are considered to be in scope for this LLD:

7 ASP.NET Core 8 Microservices: Employee, Benefits, Compensation, Claims, Documents, Payroll, Reporting — component-level design, MediatR handler structure, EF Core configuration, caching strategy, and event publishing.
4 React 18 Micro Frontend Portals: Shell Host + Employee, Manager, HR Admin, Finance Remotes — Webpack 5 Module Federation configuration, JWT Context, Axios interceptor, and role-based routing.
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS): Cluster configuration, node pool sizing, HPA settings, namespace structure, pod resource limits, network policies, and rolling deployment strategy.
Azure API Management (APIM): API policy definitions, rate limiting configuration, JWT validation policy, CORS settings, and backend service routing rules.
Data Layer: Azure SQL schema design (table structure, indexes, constraints), Azure Cosmos DB container configuration (partition keys, throughput), Azure Redis Cache key design and TTL strategy.
Azure Service Bus: Topic and subscription configuration, message schema, Dead Letter Queue handling.
Azure Event Hub: Partition configuration, consumer group design, retention settings, Serilog sink setup.
Azure Functions: Blob Trigger (OCR), Timer Trigger (scheduled reports), Queue Trigger (payroll batch) — function app configuration and bindings.
Security: Azure Key Vault secret naming conventions, Managed Identity assignments per service, network security group (NSG) rules, private endpoint configuration.
CI/CD: GitHub Actions workflow structure per service, container image build and push to ACR, AKS deployment strategy.
Monitoring: Azure Monitor alert rules, Application Insights custom metrics, log query definitions.
HA / DR: AKS multi-zone deployment, Azure SQL auto-failover group, Cosmos DB multi-region write, RTO/RPO targets, and failover procedures.
The following technical solutions are considered to be out of scope for this LLD:
Internal configuration, administration, or schema design of external payroll systems (SAP / ADP / Workday).
Mobile native application development — not in scope for Release 1.0.
Legacy HR system data migration — covered in a separate Migration Design document.
Azure Active Directory tenant configuration and Conditional Access Policy administration — managed by the Cognizant IT Security team.
Network infrastructure outside the Azure VNet (corporate WAN, on-premise network).
Third-party SLA definitions beyond the Microsoft Azure SLA framework.

The needs of Cognizant stakeholders are the basis for determining requirements. The stakeholder needs, expectations, constraints, interfaces, operational concepts, and product concepts are analyzed, harmonized, refined, and elaborated for translation into a set of customer requirements. The following are the business and functional requirements established for the Unified Rewards Platform.
Table below lists the Functional Business and Technical Requirements:

Employee Benefits Management — Employee shall be able to view and enroll in eligible benefit plans (Health Insurance, LTA, Meal Card, Flexi). Eligibility evaluated by grade, tenure, employment type, and location.
Benefits Service (ASP.NET Core 8), Azure Cosmos DB (BenefitPlans container), EligibilityCriteria Evaluator, Employee Remote MFE
Expense Claim Submission — Employee shall submit expense claims with line items and OCR-scanned receipts. Claim status transitions: DRAFT → SUBMITTED → UNDER_REVIEW.
Claims Service (state machine), Documents Service (IronOCR), Azure Blob Storage (receipts), Azure Service Bus (ClaimSubmittedEvent)
Manager Claim Approval — Manager shall review and approve or reject claims for own team within 7-business-day SLA. Rejection requires mandatory reason.
Claims Service (ClaimWorkflow), Manager Remote MFE, Azure Service Bus (ClaimApproved/RejectedEvent), Email Service
OCR Receipt Processing — System shall extract vendorName, receiptDate, and receiptAmount from uploaded receipt images with confidence scoring.
Documents Service, Azure Function (Blob Trigger), IronOCR / Tesseract.NET, OCRResult entity (Azure SQL)
Compensation Management — HR Admin shall configure grade-based compensation rules using NRules Engine. Rules compute BasicPay, HRA, Bonus, and PF per employee.
Compensation Service, NRules Engine, Azure Cosmos DB (CompensationRules container), HR Admin Remote MFE
Payroll Settlement — Approved claims shall settle in the next payroll cycle. Finalized payroll pushed to external SAP/ADP/Workday system with Polly 3× retry.
Payroll Service, Settlement handler, ClosedXML (PaySlip), IPayrollService adapter, Polly retry policy
Promotion Cycle Management — HR Admin shall create promotion cycles with eligibility criteria. Managers nominate team members. Outcomes recorded by HR Admin.
Promotions Service, PromotionEligibility evaluator, PromotionNomination entity, HR Admin Remote MFE
Compliance Reporting — Finance User shall generate Claim Summary, Compensation, and Audit reports with export to XLSX / PDF.
Reporting Service, ClosedXML, Azure Blob Storage (reports), Azure SQL (Reports, AuditEntries), Finance Remote MFE
Real-time Audit Trail — All platform actions shall be captured as immutable AuditEntry records via Azure Event Hub.
Azure Event Hub, Serilog Event Hub sink, Reporting Service (AuditEntry ingestion), Azure SQL (AuditEntries)
Role-Based Access Control — Four roles (EMPLOYEE, MANAGER, HR_ADMIN, FINANCE_USER) shall enforce access control at API and UI levels.
Azure AD JWT (RS256), APIM JWT validation policy, [Authorize] ASP.NET Core middleware, React Role Guards
Multi-Region Availability — Platform shall maintain 99.9%+ availability with auto-failover for critical data services.
AKS HPA, Azure SQL auto-failover group, Cosmos DB multi-region write, Azure Redis Zone-redundant
Benefit Deduction Integration — Approved benefit enrollments shall automatically register premium deductions with Payroll Service.
Benefits Service → Payroll Service (internal REST call), PayrollDeduction entity, Azure SQL
PaySlip Generation and Download — Employee shall download monthly PaySlip PDF post-payroll finalization.
Payroll Service, ClosedXML PDF generation, Azure Blob Storage (payslips/), Employee Remote MFE
Figure: Unified Rewards Platform — System Use Case Overview (All Actors & 7 Modules)
Figure: UC-01: User Management — Use Case Diagram
Figure: UC-02: Benefits Management — Use Case Diagram
Figure: UC-03: Compensation Management — Use Case Diagram
Figure: UC-04: Claims & Documents Management — Use Case Diagram
Figure: UC-05: Payroll Management — Use Case Diagram
Figure: UC-06: Promotions Management — Use Case Diagram
Figure: UC-07: Reporting & Audit — Use Case Diagram
Table below lists the Non-Functional Business Requirements:
High Availability — System components shall be designed for zero single-point-of-failure. Minimum 2 pods per AKS service; zone-redundant data services.
AKS HPA (min 2, max 10 pods); Azure SQL Zone-redundant; Cosmos DB multi-region; Redis Premium Zone-redundant
Performance — API response time < 500ms (P95) for synchronous endpoints; < 2s for report generation initiation.
Azure Redis Cache (TTL: 15/30/60 min); APIM caching policy; EF Core async queries; Cosmos DB auto-scale RU
Scalability — Platform shall scale horizontally to support 10,000 concurrent employees without architectural changes.
AKS HPA (CPU/memory-based); Cosmos DB auto-scale (400–4000 RU/s); Azure SQL elastic pool; Service Bus Premium
Security — All data at rest encrypted (AES-256); all data in transit encrypted (TLS 1.3); zero stored credentials.
Azure SQL TDE; Cosmos DB server-side encryption; Blob SSE; Key Vault MSI; Private VNet endpoints
Disaster Recovery — RTO: 30 minutes; RPO: 5 minutes for transactional data; RPO: 1 hour for document storage.
Azure SQL auto-failover group; Cosmos DB multi-region write; Blob GRS; AKS DR cluster (West US 2)
Audit and Compliance — Immutable audit trail for all domain actions; 30-day Event Hub retention; 7-year SQL audit record retention.
Azure Event Hub (append-only); AuditEntries (Azure SQL, immutable); ClaimStatusHistory (append-only)
Maintainability — Independent deployment per service/portal; zero-downtime rolling deployments; < 15-minute deployment time
GitHub Actions per-service pipeline; AKS rolling update strategy (maxSurge:1, maxUnavailable:0); Docker multi-stage build
Observability — Distributed tracing across all services; custom metric dashboards; PagerDuty integration for critical alerts.
Azure Application Insights (x-correlation-id propagation); Azure Monitor alert rules; Serilog structured logging
This section documents the key decisions made while preparing the design from the architecture perspective:
Platform decomposed into 7 independently deployable ASP.NET Core 8 microservices, each owning its data domain.
Domain isolation enables independent scaling, deployment, and fault isolation. Prevents cascading failures across domains.
All business logic implemented as MediatR Commands and Queries. Zero business logic in Controllers.
Strict separation of concerns; Commands (writes) and Queries (reads) are independently testable and auditable.
Webpack 5 ModuleFederationPlugin used to lazy-load 4 role-specific Remote Apps from a Shell Host at runtime.
Independent frontend deployment without Shell App changes. Each portal has its own CI/CD pipeline and versioning.
Compensation rules stored as NRules formulas in Cosmos DB; evaluated at runtime without redeployment.
HR Admin can update grade-based CTC rules without developer involvement or code deployment.
Claims Service publishes ClaimApproved event to Azure Service Bus; Payroll Service subscribes to trigger Settlement.
Full decoupling of Claims and Payroll domains. Payroll Service can fail/restart without impacting claim processing.
Azure SQL for transactional domains (Claims, Payroll, Employees); Cosmos DB for flexible-schema domains (Benefits, Compensation).
SQL for ACID-compliant transactional data; Cosmos DB for high-read, variable-schema document data. Right tool per domain.
JWT Bearer token stored in React Context (in-memory heap). Never stored in localStorage or sessionStorage.
Prevents XSS-based token theft. Token lost on page refresh forces re-authentication via Azure AD — intentional security design.
All Azure service connections use MSI (Managed Identity). No stored passwords or API keys in configuration.
Eliminates credential exposure risk in config files, Kubernetes secrets, or CI/CD pipelines. Key rotation without downtime.
Private VNet for all Data Services
Azure SQL, Cosmos DB, Redis, Blob, Key Vault, Service Bus, Event Hub accessible only via Private Link within VNet.
No public internet exposure of data tier. Defence-in-depth: even if APIM is compromised, data tier is unreachable.
exponential back-off) + circuit breaker + Dead Letter Queue.
transient failures. Polly prevents cascading failure; DLQ enables manual recovery.

This section provides the low-level technical design for the Unified Rewards Platform. It covers solution architecture, logical design, component configuration, server roles, sizing, software prerequisites, and detailed component specifications for each layer of the platform.
The Unified Rewards Platform is a cloud-native, microservices-based HR Technology platform deployed exclusively on Microsoft Azure. The architecture follows a 5-tier model:
Figure: Solution Architecture Overview — 5-Layer System Context

All user interactions originate from web browsers. Requests traverse Azure Front Door (WAF inspection, CDN caching for static assets) before reaching the React MFE portals served from Azure Static Web Apps. API calls from portals flow through Azure APIM, which enforces JWT validation and rate limiting before routing to the target AKS microservice. Services communicate with data stores exclusively via Private Link within the Azure VNet — no data service has a public endpoint. Asynchronous domain events (clai
m lifecycle) flow through Azure Service Bus. All audit events stream to Azure Event Hub for compliance ingestion by the Reporting Service.
The logical design describes the internal structure of each microservice and the interactions between them. Each of the 7 microservices follows the same 4-layer internal structure:
Controller Layer: Minimal ASP.NET Core 8 controllers. Only responsibility is receiving HTTP requests and dispatching via ISender.Send(command/query). No business logic.

Application Layer (MediatR): IRequestHandler<Command,Result> for writes; IRequestHandler<Query,Result> for reads. FluentValidation Pipeline Behavior validates before handler execution. INotificationHandler for domain event processing.
Domain Layer: Entity classes, Value Objects, Domain Events, and Business Rules. No infrastructure dependencies.
Infrastructure Layer: EF Core 8 DbContext (Repository pattern), external service adapters (IPayrollService, IEmailService, IOCRService, IStorageService, IAuditEventEmitter), and Azure SDK clients.
C# domain models, enums, value objects
Entities (Claim, Employee, etc.), ClaimStatus enum, ClaimWorkflow state machine
EF Core 8 + Azure SQL / Cosmos SDK
Azure Service Bus SDK + Event Hub SDK
IPayrollService, IEmailService, IOCRService (local mock + cloud impl)
Figure: Logical Architecture Diagram — Horizontal Tiers & Cross-cutting Concerns
The following design components are identified for the Unified Rewards Platform. Each component is independently configurable and deployed:
SQL: Employees+Users tables; Cache TTL: 15 min; IMemoryCache key: employee:{id}
Cosmos container: BenefitPlans; Cache TTL: 30 min; EligibilityCriteria evaluator
Cosmos container: CompensationRules; NRules compiled at startup; Cache TTL: 60 min
SQL: Claims+Workflow+History; State machine; publishes 4 Service Bus topics
Blob container: documents/; OCR via Azure Function Blob Trigger; IronOCR
SQL: PayrollRecords+Settlements; ClosedXML PaySlip; Polly 3× retry; DLQ
SQL: Reports+AuditEntries; LINQ aggregation; XLSX/PDF export; internal only
Host config: remoteEntry.js per remote; JWT Context; React Router v6
Routes: /reports, /audit; FINANCE_USER role guard; ClosedXML export trigger
100 req/min rate limit; JWT RS256 validation; CORS; /api/v1/ routing; Private Link → AKS
2 node pools: system (Standard_D2s_v3) + user (Standard_D4s_v3); HPA 2–10 pods
General Purpose GP_Gen5_4; Zone-redundant; Geo-replica West US 2; PITR 35 days
Zone-redundant; TLS 6380; no persistence (AOF/RDB disabled); eviction: allkeys-lru
Containers: documents/, payslips/, reports/; Soft delete: 14 days; Private endpoint
4 topics: claims/submitted, claims/approved, claims/rejected, claims/settled; TTL: 7 days
1 Event Hub: audit-events; 32 partitions; 30-day retention; 1 consumer group per service
Secrets: jwt-signing-key, sql-conn-str, cosmos-conn-str, redis-conn-str, payroll-api-key
WAF policy: OWASP 3.2; DDoS Standard; SSL cert: *.unifiedrewards.cognizant.com
App registration; PKCE flow; JWT RS256; MFA Conditional Access; 4 app roles assigned
AKS User Node Pool (7 microservices + 3 Azure Functions)
Azure SQL Database — Secondary (Read replica, auto-failover)
Azure Functions App (OCR + Reports + Payroll batch)
GRS; Hot tier; up to 5 PB
4.7 APPLIANCE DETAILS (ADJUST AS APPLICABLE FOR APPLIANCES)
The table below lists all software prerequisites for each component:
8.0.x LTS — pre-installed in Docker base image (mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0)
v1.4+ — required for Private VNet integration and NSG enforcement
AKS Workload Identity — required for MSI-based access to Key Vault, Blobs, SQL
3.x — auto-instrumentation for distributed tracing (via Helm chart)
0.15.x — via NuGet; rules compiled from Cosmos DB formulas at startup
12.x — via NuGet; all services use CQRS pipeline
11.x — via NuGet; pipeline behavior validates all commands/queries
2024.x — via NuGet; requires IronOCR license key (from Key Vault)
8.x — via NuGet; retry + circuit breaker for external payroll REST calls
3.x + 3.x — structured logging pipeline; cloud sink: EventHub
12.x — Blob Trigger binding for OCR function
20.x LTS — build-time only; runtime is browser
1.x — HTTP client with Bearer token interceptor
High Level Design and Architecture Document
REST API contracts for all 7 microservices
Azure deployment topology with VNet and regions
Entity-relationship diagrams for all 7 data domains
End-to-end sequence flows for 6 key business processes
The component and configuration details comprise the AKS application layer, Azure data services, messaging infrastructure, and security components. Configurations are managed via:
Kubernetes ConfigMaps: Non-sensitive configuration (service URLs, feature flags, cache TTL values, environment names).
Azure Key Vault Secrets: All sensitive configuration (connection strings, JWT signing key, external API keys). Fetched at pod startup via Workload Identity MSI.
Environment Variables (non-sensitive): ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT, AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID (Workload Identity).
Kubernetes Resource Limits per pod: requests: cpu=200m, memory=256Mi; limits: cpu=1000m, memory=512Mi.
AKS HPA configuration: minReplicas=2, maxReplicas=10, targetCPUUtilizationPercentage=70.
EF Core migrations: Applied automatically at service startup in non-Production environments; manual migration approval required in Production
The Unified Rewards Platform is designed for 99.9%+ availability across all tiers. The following HA mechanisms are implemented:
minReplicas=2, maxReplicas=10; nodes spread across 3 AZs; RollingUpdate (maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0)
Deployed across 3 Availability Zones in East US 2
Primary (EUS2) → Secondary (WUS2); auto-failover grace period: 30 min
Write: East US 2 + West US 2; Strong consistency for writes; Eventual for reads
Premium P1 with zone-redundancy; no persistence (data reconstructed from DB on restart)
RA-GRS: 6 synchronous copies across 2 paired regions
Geo-disaster recovery: primary (EUS2) ↔ alias; manual failover
WAF + CDN across 150+ PoPs globally; automatic health probes
Scales from 0 to 200 instances on demand; no manual HA configuration

The DR strategy for the Unified Rewards Platform is based on Azure paired regions (East US 2 → West US 2). Active-passive DR model with automated failover for data services and manual failover for application tier.
The platform uses the following DR tiers based on component criticality:
Auto-failover group; secondary in West US 2 (readable)
Multi-region write; West US 2 as secondary write region; automatic failover
DR AKS cluster in West US 2 (pre-provisioned, stopped); activated via GitHub Actions DR pipeline
Geo-disaster recovery alias; manual failover to West US 2 namespace
RA-GRS; read access to West US 2 during primary outage; Microsoft-managed failover
Zone-redundant within East US 2; contents replicated to paired region automatically
Backup/restore to West US 2 via ARM template; DNS cut-over via Front Door routing rules
Auto-replicated globally via Azure CDN; no specific DR action needed
< 30 minutes for application tier; < 30 seconds for data tier auto-failover
Time from incident declaration to service restoration in West US 2
< 5 minutes for transactional data (Azure SQL); 0 for Cosmos DB writes
Maximum data loss in the event of primary region failure
< 1 hour for full platform recovery including application tier
Measured from incident ticket creation to health check passing
Azure Monitor availability alert; monthly uptime report
Frequency: DR failover test conducted quarterly (once per calendar quarter).
Scope: Full failover test includes: AKS DR cluster activation, APIM re-pointing, SQL auto-failover group test, Cosmos DB failover verification, Service Bus alias switch.

Test Procedure: (1) Declare test DR window (off-hours); (2) Trigger SQL auto-failover group test; (3) Scale up West US 2 AKS DR cluster; (4) Update DNS/Front Door routing to West US 2 APIM; (5) Run smoke test suite against DR environment; (6) Validate AuditEntry continuity in DR region.
Success Criteria: All 9 key API endpoints respond correctly in DR region; RTO < 30 minutes achieved; No data loss confirmed (Cosmos DB); PayrollRecord PITR recovery test successful.
Rollback: After DR test, revert SQL failover group back to East US 2 primary; scale down West US 2 AKS nodes.
Restore via Azure Portal: point-in-time restore to new/existing server; update connection string if new server
Self-service restore via Azure Portal; restore to new Cosmos DB account; re-point service connection
Recover soft-deleted blob: Azure Portal / Azure CLI az storage blob undelete
Key Vault secrets auto-restored in DR region; no manual action for key recovery
On every CI/CD push (triggered by GitHub Actions)
Re-pull latest image from ACR; redeploy via kubectl apply
Kubernetes manifest backup via GitOps (GitHub repository)
Every infrastructure change committed to GitHub
kubectl apply -f k8s/ from GitHub repository to re-create cluster resources
The following naming conventions and numbering schemes are applied across the platform:
All 7 services deployed to single namespace per env
Used by HPA and network policies
Semantic versioning; latest tag used for dev only
No custom schemas; domain separation by table naming
One container per domain in Cosmos DB
TTL appended at write time per domain
One workflow file per service per action (build/deploy)
Propagated across all service calls for end-to-end tracing

The Unified Rewards Platform is a fully cloud-native deployment on Microsoft Azure. Physical rack elevation diagrams are not applicable as all infrastructure is hosted on Azure-managed physical hardware (shared, multi-tenant datacenters). The logical equivalent is the Azure Availability Zone layout described in the Deployment View.
Figure: Azure Regional Deployment Layout — Multi-Region Topology
The following section provides physical connectivity and specifications for all major Azure infrastructure components:
Azure CNI; NSG: allow kubectl from DevOps IPs only
3 nodes, 1 per AZ; kube-system + monitoring workloads
Min 5 nodes; HPA scales to 10; all 7 microservices + Functions
TDE enabled; 256 GB storage; auto-grow; PITR 35 days
Read-only replica; auto-failover group; lag < 5 min
No disk persistence; eviction: allkeys-lru; maxmemory-policy set
4 topics; 10 MB message size; Geo-DR alias configured

Note: All physical servers (AKS nodes) are ephemeral by design — no stateful data stored on node local disk. All state lives in Azure-managed data services listed above. Node OS disk is used for container layers only.

This section provides detailed specifications for all integration points of the Unified Rewards Platform including database configuration, backup requirements, monitoring, logging, port matrix, service accounts, and capacity planning.
The platform uses two database technologies. Detailed configuration:
Azure AD MSI (Workload Identity) — no SQL auth
Azure AD MSI — no connection string keys
All regions are read/write in multi-region config
150 connections per EF Core service pool
HTTP-based SDK — no connection pool limit

Tables by domain: dbo.Users, dbo.Employees, dbo.Claims, dbo.ClaimLineItems, dbo.ClaimWorkflows, dbo.ClaimStatusHistories, dbo.Documents, dbo.Receipts, dbo.OCRResults, dbo.PayrollRecords, dbo.Settlements, dbo.PaySlips, dbo.Reports, dbo.AuditEntries
Code-first migrations; per-service DbContext; UseAzureSql(); connection from Key Vault
Cosmos DB SDK directly (no EF Core); per-container repository pattern
Primary keys: UNIQUEIDENTIFIER (NEWSEQUENTIALID()); FK indexes on employee_id, claim_id, payroll_id; Filtered index on Claims.status for active claims query
Auto-indexed all properties; custom composite index on (grade, validFrom) for CompensationRules
Full: Sunday 02:00 UTC; Diff: every 12h; Log: every 5–10 min
Restore to same or new server in same region (or DR region)
Restore to new Cosmos DB account; re-point service
Continuous geo-replication; soft delete trigger on deletion
Undelete via Azure CLI; RA-GRS read access during outage
Auto-available in DR region; no manual restore needed
Azure Container Registry — built on each CI/CD push
30 most recent tags per image repository
Re-pull from ACR; redeploy via kubectl
Re-apply from Git; no data risk (non-sensitive)

The Unified Rewards Platform uses Azure Defender for Cloud (Microsoft Defender) as the primary threat detection and anti-malware solution. No traditional AV agent is deployed on AKS nodes (Kubernetes containers are ephemeral and immutable by design).
The following security controls provide anti-malware equivalent protection:
All Docker images in Azure Container Registry (ACR)
Scan on every push to ACR; block deployment if HIGH/CRITICAL CVE detected
Alerts on: privilege escalation, suspicious process, crypto mining, port scanning
Alerts on: SQL injection, anomalous access, brute force; Email alert to security team
Scan uploaded files on Blob Trigger event; quarantine malware detections before OCR processing
Azure Firewall / NSG Flow Logs + Defender for Cloud
All VNet traffic across Application and Data subnets
Alert on: unexpected outbound connections, DDoS attempts, lateral movement
N/A (container-based — no persistent files on nodes)
Containers are read-only filesystem; /tmp is ephemeral; no persistent files to exclude
Azure Monitor + Application Insights provides full-stack observability. The following table lists key monitoring services and parameters:
Average CPU usage across all pods per service
Scale out via HPA (automatic); PagerDuty alert if > 90%
Average memory usage across all pods per
PagerDuty alert; pod OOM kills trigger incident
95th percentile API response time across all APIM routes
Azure Monitor alert → PagerDuty high priority
Percentage of API responses with 5xx status code
PagerDuty critical alert; auto-rollback trigger in GitHub Actions
Alert to DBA team; consider scale-up to GP_Gen5_8
Alert to DevOps; investigate connection leak
Request Units consumed vs provisioned (400–4000 RU/s auto-scale)
Alert to DevOps; increase max RU/s if sustained
Percentage of cache reads served from cache (not DB)
Investigation alert: cache invalidation issue or cold start
Unprocessed messages in any topic subscription
Alert to DevOps; check subscriber service health
Failed messages in DLQ (Payroll Service → External Payroll)
Critical alert — manual intervention required for payroll push failure
Lag between Event Hub publish and Reporting Service consumption
Alert to DevOps; check Reporting Service health
Key Vault operation latency or throttling events
Alert to DevOps; review MSI request patterns
Time for Azure Function OCR Blob Trigger to complete
Alert to DevOps; check IronOCR performance / file size
Failed PaySlip PDF generation (ClosedXML) per payroll cycle
Critical alert to Payroll team; PaySlip regeneration required
Unhandled exceptions logged by Serilog across all services
Alert to respective service development team
All 7 microservices use Serilog v3.x for structured logging. Log pipeline is environment-specific:
Azure Application Insights (via Serilog AI Sink)
JSON — enriched with CorrelationId, ServiceName, Environment
Azure Event Hub (primary audit sink) + Azure Application
Event Hub: 30 days; App Insights: 90 days
JSON structured — AuditEvent schema for Event Hub; ITelemetry for
Logging enrichment properties added to every log entry:
x-correlation-id: UUID propagated from HTTP request header across all service calls (set by APIM on inbound if absent).
ServiceName: "employee-service", "claims-service", etc. — set in appsettings.json per deployment.
UserId: extracted from JWT sub claim by Serilog enricher middleware.
MachineName: AKS pod name (from Kubernetes downward API env var).
Figure: Logging & Messaging Architecture — Event Hub, Service Bus & App Insights
The table below lists all source-to-destination port definitions for the Unified Rewards Platform:
WAF inspection applied; HTTP 80 redirected to 443
CDN edge to origin; TLS only
Private Link; APIM → AKS via internal IP 10.0.2.x
Private Endpoint 10.0.4.6; MSI auth; SDK HTTPS
Private Endpoint 10.0.4.7; TLS only (non-TLS port 6379 disabled)
Private Endpoint 10.0.4.8; MSI auth; SDK HTTPS
Private Endpoint 10.0.4.10; MSI auth; AMQP over TLS
Blob Trigger binding; same Private Endpoint 10.0.4.8
Timer/Queue trigger functions write to SQL; MSI auth
Via AKS NAT Gateway public IP; API Key auth; Polly retry
APIM fetches JWKS for JWT validation; Internet egress allowed
Push Docker images; OIDC federation (no stored credentials)
kubectl apply via AKS API server; OIDC auth; NSG: allow GitHub IPs
Authorized IP range restricted to Cognizant VPN egress IPs only
The Unified Rewards Platform uses Azure Managed Identity (Workload Identity) for all service-to-Azure-service authentication. No shared service account passwords are used.
Employee Service → Azure SQL (urp_db), Key Vault secret reads,
dbo.Users + dbo.Employees; Key Vault: Secret Get; Redis: READ+WRITE
Benefits Service → Azure Cosmos DB (BenefitPlans container), Key Vault, Redis
Cosmos DB: Contributor on BenefitPlans + BenefitEnrollments; Key Vault: Secret Get; Redis: READ+WRITE
Compensation Service → Azure Cosmos DB (CompensationRules), Key Vault, Redis
Cosmos DB: Contributor on CompensationRules container; Key Vault: Secret Get (IronOCR key); Redis: READ+WRITE
Claims Service → Azure SQL (Claims tables), Service Bus (publish), Event Hub (audit), Key Vault
SQL: db_datareader + db_datawriter on Claims schema; Service Bus: Send on 4 topics; Event Hub: Send; Key Vault: Secret Get
Documents Service → Azure Blob Storage (documents/ container), Azure SQL (Documents+Receipts+OCRResults), Key Vault
Blob: Storage Blob Data Contributor on documents/ container; SQL: db_datareader + db_datawriter; Key Vault: Secret Get
Payroll Service → Azure SQL (Payroll tables), Blob (payslips/), Service Bus (subscribe), Event Hub, Key Vault
SQL: db_datareader + db_datawriter on Payroll schema; Blob: Storage Blob Data Contributor on payslips/; Service Bus: Listen on claims/approved; Key Vault: Secret Get (external payroll API key)
Reporting Service → Azure SQL (read-only: all tables), Blob (reports/), Event Hub (consume), Key Vault
SQL: db_datareader on all tables (read-only — no writes to transactional tables); Blob: Storage Blob Data Contributor on reports/; Event Hub: Listen (AuditEntry ingestion); Key Vault: Secret Get
Azure Functions App → Azure Blob (Blob Trigger), Azure SQL (Timer/Queue write), Key Vault
Blob: Storage Blob Data Reader on documents/ (Blob Trigger); SQL: db_datawriter (OCRResults, Reports); Key Vault: Secret Get
GitHub Actions CI/CD → Azure Container Registry (image push), AKS (kubectl deploy)
ACR: AcrPush role; AKS: Azure Kubernetes Service Contributor role (namespace-scoped to urp-prod / urp-uat)
Azure Monitor Agent on AKS nodes → Log Analytics Workspace
Log Analytics: Monitoring Metrics Publisher; Reader on all urp-prod-rg resources
Role Based Account assignments for platform administration:
Owner on urp-prod-rg; AKS Cluster Admin; Key Vault Administrator
Full platform administration; break-glass access; restricted to 2–3 persons with PIM (Privileged Identity Management)
Contributor on urp-prod-rg (excluding Key Vault); AKS Cluster User; ACR Push/Pull
CI/CD pipeline management; AKS deployment; image builds. No access to Key Vault secrets.
Reader on urp-uat-rg; AKS Cluster User (uat namespace only); Azure SQL (uat db — db_owner)
UAT environment access for debugging; read-only on Production; no access to Production data
Reader on urp-uat-rg; Azure Static Web Apps Contributor (uat)
UAT portal deployment; read-only on Production resources
SQL Server Contributor on urp-prod-sql-eus2; db_owner on urp_db (prod)
Schema changes; index optimization; PITR restore execution; read-only Cosmos DB access
Security Reader + Microsoft Defender for Cloud alerts; Key Vault Administrator
Security monitoring; WAF rule management; Key Vault key rotation; Defender for Cloud alert triage
Reader on all urp-prod-rg resources; Application Insights Reader; Log Analytics Reader
Read-only monitoring access; log viewing for incident investigation; no configuration changes
Current deployment capacity and available scaling options:
5 nodes × Standard_D4s_v3 (4 vCPU / 16 GB each) = 20 vCPU / 80 GB total
Add nodes to user node pool (max 20); Upgrade to Standard_D8s_v3 (8 vCPU)
Cluster Autoscaler: node CPU > 80% for > 10 min
2 min → 10 max per service (7 services × 2 min
range; increase max in HPA manifest
GP_Gen5_4 (4 vCores, 20.4 GB RAM, 256 GB storage)
Scale to GP_Gen5_8 (8 vCores) via Azure Portal — zero downtime; storage auto-grows to 4 TB
Increase max RU/s to 10,000 RU/s via SDK or Portal; no downtime
Azure Monitor: RU/s > 80% of max auto-scale ceiling
Scale to P2 (13 GB) or P3 (26 GB); cluster sharding up to 10 shards
Memory usage > 80% for > 15 min; manual trigger
Add Messaging Units (up to 16 MU) via Portal — no downtime
StorageV2 GRS (up to 5 PB per account)
Add more storage accounts if > 4.75 PB; unlikely in foreseeable future
Premium tier: 4000 req/sec (theoretical); rate limit: 100 req/min per subscription
Scale Premium units (add Scale units via Portal); increase rate limits in policy
Consumption plan — scales 0 to 200 instances automatically
Upgrade to Premium plan for VNet integration and pre-warmed instances if cold-start > 1 sec
Cold-start latency > 1 sec on Blob Trigger OCR function
High Level Design and Architecture Document covering all 11 architectural views
REST API contracts for all 7 microservices with request/response schemas
Platform functional and non-functional requirements; 26 clarification items
Layered logical architecture with all tiers and cross-cutting concerns
Azure VNet topology with private endpoints, AZ layout, and region distribution
Entity-relationship diagrams for all 7 domains with table definitions
End-to-end sequence diagrams for Claim Submission, Enrollment,
Class-level domain models for all 7 bounded contexts
Component diagrams covering System Overview, Frontend, Backend, Data, Messaging, Security
Application architecture and design security standard
Azure architecture best practices: Reliability, Security, Cost, Performance, Operations
Web application security risk framework — applied to APIM WAF policy
Table 5. Functional Business and Technical Requirements
Table 15. AKS and Azure Service HA Configuration
Table 20. Azure SQL vs Cosmos DB Configuration

Initial baseline created on 23-May-2026 by Senior Solution Architect. Full LLD covering all 7 microservices, 4 MFE portals, Azure infrastructure, HA/DR strategy, monitoring, logging, port matrix, service accounts, capacity planning, and backup requirements for the Unified Rewards Platform.
